evidence_quotes.quote: This is because in the gaming industry, the practice of selling games with built-in kill switches and using them if necessary is widespread.
When you buy a video game these days, you can no longer be sure that the game will be maintained until it loses its functionality, e.g. through fault or wear.
for example, the point with the gambling elements in digital games for children.
Ownership is placed on the head, and in many cases the consumer is not even properly informed when buying.

paragraphs.text: Dear EU, I would be delighted if you would look at the objectives of the Stop Killing Games Initiative and consider adding one or more points to your legislative proposal in order to address the problems raised by the initiative together with those already listed by you. I am now the most serious problem for me personally, which criticises the initiative: When you buy a video game these days, you can no longer be sure that the game will be maintained until it loses its functionality, e.g. through fault or wear. This is because in the gaming industry, the practice of selling games with built-in kill switches and using them if necessary is widespread. That means: more and more games work only if they regularly (e.g. hourly) contact a manufacturer’s server in order to be ‘authorised’ by the manufacturer to function normally. If no authorisation is received, the game ends and cannot be played further. This mechanism has already been used by companies to make old versions of popular games unusable, in order to almost force consumers to buy the new edition. In my view, the unacceptably aggressive capitalist practices, which are not only a predication on the consumer, but a permissive interference in his private life through the deliberate destruction of an item acquired in accordance with established purchasing and selling practices, and which – again according to common understanding – would have been the property of the consumer. Ownership is placed on the head, and in many cases the consumer is not even properly informed when buying. In addition to this problem, there are other issues which I also consider important, such as the destruction of cultural goods by certain capitalist practices of the gaming brache. However, instead of writing a manifesto here, I would recommend that the organisers of the Stop Killing Games Initiative be interviewed about the problems identified so far and the cases documented so far. Finally, I would like to say that I also very much welcome the points already listed in your legislative proposal: for example, the point with the gambling elements in digital games for children. I have long seen this as a problem and would very much welcome the EU’s stronger regulation and, where appropriate, bans such harmful excesses. Thank you!
